points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: TVK's policy is that true social justice means every community receives proportional representation in the appropriate measure. To ensure this, a Caste Survey will be conducted.
points.title: 1 Proportional Representation

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu government to conduct caste survey
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government announced it would conduct a survey to collect caste-wise data to ensure social justice, following the Bihar model.

plan_existence.notes: While the Bihar government completed a caste survey and the TN government has expressed intent/passed resolutions, a comprehensive, completed survey for proportional representation is not currently active in Tamil Nadu.

legal.comments: The power to conduct a 'Census' rests solely with the Union Government under the Census Act, 1948. States can conduct 'Surveys' under the Collection of Statistics Act, but these are often challenged in court regarding privacy and jurisdiction.

administrative.comments: Tamil Nadu has a robust administrative machinery (village assistants, teachers, revenue officials) capable of conducting door-to-door surveys.

timeline.comments: A survey can typically be completed within 12-18 months if legal hurdles are cleared.

political.comments: High demand from MBC/OBC groups, but potentially polarizing among different community blocks depending on the results.

feasibility.overall_comments: The proposal is administratively feasible but faces significant legal hurdles regarding the distinction between a 'census' and a 'survey'. Implementation depends on navigating Supreme Court precedents.
key_risks: Legal challenge in Supreme Court over jurisdiction
Data privacy concerns
Political backlash from communities whose share might decrease

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: A resolution will be passed in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly demanding the fair and full financial share due to Tamil Nadu from the taxes it contributes to the Union Government. Not stopping with merely passing the resolution, a Special Legal Committee will be constituted to take the fight up to the Supreme Court and legally argue for Tamil Nadu's financial share. Every rupee of revenue that should come to Tamil Nadu will be properly documented, and a report on the Union Government's injustice regarding Tamil Nadu's finances will be published. Furthermore, obtaining the fair financial share due to Tamil Nadu from the Union Government will be ensured.
points.title: 2 Demanding a Fair Share of Finances for Tamil Nadu

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 2 Demanding a Fair Share of Finances for Tamil Nadu
A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Kerala moves Supreme Court against Centre over borrowing limits
evidence.snippet: The State of Kerala filed a suit under Article 131 of the Constitution challenging the Union's interference in its fiscal autonomy.

plan_existence.notes: The TN government already frequently passes resolutions and submits memoranda to the Finance Commission. The proposal to form a 'Special Legal Committee' for the Supreme Court follows the precedent set by Kerala.

legal.comments: Article 131 allows states to sue the Union. However, the Finance Commission's recommendations are generally considered binding once accepted by the President, making legal challenges to devolution formulas difficult.

administrative.comments: Setting up a committee and publishing reports is well within the state's administrative capacity.

timeline.comments: Resolutions and committees can be formed within months.

political.comments: Strong public support in TN for 'state rights' and 'fair share' narratives.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as a political and administrative action, though the legal outcome in the Supreme Court regarding tax devolution is uncertain and historically difficult for states to win.
key_risks: Protracted legal battle with no guaranteed outcome
Strained Union-State relations affecting other grants
Constitutional limits on challenging Finance Commission formulas

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: The TVK government will stand firm in securing Tamil Nadu's finances from the Union Government. In particular, it will strongly insist that 70 percent of the total GST tax revenue be given to the states. A proper GST compensation amount will also be demanded so that states are relieved from revenue losses.
points.title: 3 Rightful and Fair Share for Tamil Nadu from GST Tax Revenue

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 3 Rightful and Fair Share for Tamil Nadu from GST Tax Revenue
A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: GST Council Structure and Revenue Sharing
evidence.snippet: GST is currently split 50:50 between Center (CGST) and State (SGST). IGST is also shared based on Council recommendations.

plan_existence.notes: The current system is a 50/50 split. Demanding 70% for states would require a fundamental change to the GST Act and the consensus of the GST Council.

fiscal.comments: A shift to 70% for states would cause a massive revenue deficit for the Union Government, which relies on its 50% share for central schemes and defense. No specific cost-benefit analysis is provided.

legal.comments: Requires an amendment to the GST Act and potentially the Constitution, which needs a 2/3rd majority in Parliament and ratification by half the states. A single state cannot implement this.

administrative.comments: The GST network (GSTN) can technically handle different percentage splits, but the policy change is the barrier.

timeline.comments: Unlikely to be achieved in 5 years given the need for national consensus in the GST Council.

political.comments: Would be supported by other 'producer' states but opposed by the Union and potentially 'consumer' states if it affects the overall pool.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically a demand rather than a state-executable plan. It is fiscally and legally improbable as it requires the Union Government to voluntarily surrender 20% of its current GST revenue share.
key_risks: Lack of consensus in the GST Council
Union Government's refusal to amend the GST Act
Massive fiscal gap for central government operations

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: It is because education (including medical education) is on the Union Government's common list that impositions such as the new education policy and the NEET exam controversy cause educational obstacles. Therefore, appropriate steps will be taken to move education (including medical education) from the Union Government's common list to the state list. In this, appropriate measures will be taken to bring a constitutional amendment in Parliament in coordination with like-minded parties. A legal battle will also be pursued by filing a review petition in the Supreme Court on this issue.
points.title: 4 Appropriate Action to Move Education to the State List!

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 4 Appropriate Action to Move Education to the State List!
A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: 42nd Amendment, 1976
evidence.snippet: Education was moved from the State List to the Concurrent List by the 42nd Amendment Act, 1976.

plan_existence.notes: Education is currently in the Concurrent List (Entry 25). Tamil Nadu has passed several resolutions to move it back to the State List, but no such change has occurred.

legal.comments: Moving a subject from the Concurrent List to the State List requires a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368, necessitating a two-thirds majority in both Houses of Parliament and ratification by at least half of the State Legislatures.

administrative.comments: Tamil Nadu has a fully functional education department and previously managed education as a state subject.

timeline.comments: Highly unlikely within 5 years as it depends on the political will of the Union Government and other states.

political.comments: While popular in TN, there is no national consensus among other major states or the ruling central parties to decentralize education to this extent.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a constitutional aspiration rather than a feasible state-level policy. The state government has no unilateral power to move education to the State List.
key_risks: Failure to get 2/3rd majority in Parliament
Rejection by other state legislatures
Supreme Court may uphold the current status as part of the basic structure of federalism

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: TVK strongly opposes the redrawing of parliamentary constituencies on the basis of population. To prevent South Indian states from being severely affected by delimitation, appropriate steps will be taken to bring a constitutional amendment.
points.title: 5 Protecting Tamil Nadu's Rights in Constituency Delimitation

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 5 Protecting Tamil Nadu's Rights in Constituency Delimitation
A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Stalin vs Centre: Why is Tamil Nadu protesting the delimitation bill
evidence.snippet: The Union government tabled the Delimitation Bill 2026 in April 2026, which seeks to redraw constituencies based on population, potentially reducing Tamil Nadu's Lok Sabha seats from 39 to 31.

plan_existence.notes: While the delimitation process is a central mandate under Article 82, the specific 'protection' or 'constitutional amendment' to freeze seats based on 1971 census levels (as currently exists until 2026) is a political demand, not an active scheme.

legal.comments: Constituency delimitation is a federal subject under Articles 81 and 82. A state government has no legal authority to amend the Constitution of India; it requires a two-thirds majority in both houses of Parliament.

administrative.comments: The state can pass resolutions and lead a coalition of states to lobby the Union, which is administratively feasible but lacks executive power to enforce.

timeline.comments: The delimitation exercise is scheduled to begin after the first census post-2026, making the window for intervention very narrow.

political.comments: High resonance in Tamil Nadu as it crosses party lines; the DMK and other regional parties share this stance, creating a unified state front.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a political stance rather than a deliverable state-level scheme. While it aligns with regional sentiment, the legal power to implement a constitutional amendment rests entirely with the Union Parliament, making state-level implementation impossible.
key_risks: Lack of legislative competence at the state level
Dependence on a 2/3rd majority in the Union Parliament
Potential conflict with northern states that stand to gain seats

Tamil Nadu two language policy vs NEP 2020 status
Tamil Nadu NEET abolition bill status 2026
One Nation One Election status India April 2026

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: TVK strongly opposes the Union Government's 'One Nation, One Election' plan, which weakens state autonomy and the federal principle. Therefore, appropriate efforts will be made to have the One Nation One Election draft bill withdrawn.
points.title: 6 Opposition to One Nation One Election

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 6 Opposition to One Nation One Election
A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: One Nation, One Election - Wikipedia
evidence.snippet: The Constitution (129th Amendment) Bill, 2024 was introduced in Lok Sabha in December 2024 and referred to a Joint Parliamentary Committee, which was extended to the Monsoon Session 2026.

plan_existence.notes: The 'One Nation, One Election' plan is a proposal by the Union Government currently under legislative scrutiny. The TVK promise is to oppose and seek withdrawal of this central bill.

legal.comments: The withdrawal of a Union Bill is the prerogative of the Union Cabinet and Parliament. A state government cannot legally force the withdrawal of central legislation.

administrative.comments: The state can refuse to provide administrative support for simultaneous polls, but this would lead to a constitutional crisis under Article 356.

timeline.comments: The Union government aims for implementation by 2029; the 2026 state government would have a limited window to influence the JPC or Parliament.

political.comments: Strongly supported by regional parties in TN who fear the overshadowing of local issues by national narratives.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a policy of resistance against a central move. Its success depends entirely on national-level political arithmetic and the ability of the party to influence the Union government, rather than any direct state executive action.
key_risks: Constitutional deadlock between State and Union
Limited influence of a single state party in the Union Parliament
Potential for Article 356 invocation if state refuses to comply with central directives

search_queries: Tamil Nadu government stance on constituency delimitation 2026
Tamil Nadu two language policy vs NEP 2020 status
Tamil Nadu NEET abolition bill status 2026
One Nation One Election status India April 2026

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: The three-language education scheme which the Union Government is attempting to impose through the new education policy will severely harm Tamil Nadu's linguistic heritage and the quality of education. On behalf of TVK, a resolution will be passed in the Legislative Assembly opposing the Union Government's new education policy. A special inter-state committee will be formed to work jointly with other state governments that share this view. It will be urged that all states in India implement a two-language policy with their respective mother tongue and English as the link language.
points.title: 7 Safeguarding the Two-Language Policy

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu edu policy vs NEP: Two-language formula retained
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu's State Education Policy (SEP), unveiled in August 2025, reiterates the state's long-standing two-language policy (Tamil and English) in direct opposition to the NEP 2020.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu has officially followed a two-language policy since 1968. The promise to pass a resolution and form an inter-state committee is a new tactical addition to an existing policy stance.

fiscal.comments: The Union government has already withheld approximately Rs 2,152 crore in Samagra Shiksha funds from Tamil Nadu due to its refusal to adopt the NEP's three-language formula.

legal.comments: Education is in the Concurrent List. While the state can set its own curriculum and language policy, the Union can use financial leverage (grants) to enforce national standards.

administrative.comments: Passing a resolution in the Assembly and forming a committee are standard executive and legislative functions that are easily achievable.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately upon forming the government.

political.comments: The two-language policy is a cornerstone of Tamil identity politics and enjoys near-unanimous support across the state's political spectrum.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it continues a 50-year-old state consensus. The primary challenge is not implementation, but the resulting fiscal friction with the Union government over withheld education grants.
key_risks: Continued withholding of central education funds
Limited interest from other states in adopting a strict two-language policy
Legal challenges regarding the mandatory nature of Tamil in private/central schools

search_queries: Tamil Nadu government stance on constituency delimitation 2026
Tamil Nadu two language policy vs NEP 2020 status
Tamil Nadu NEET abolition bill status 2026
One Nation One Election status India April 2026

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: On behalf of TVK, continued pressure will be placed on the Union Government to abolish the NEET exam.
points.title: 8 Abolition of NEET Exam

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Admission to Undergraduate Medical Degree Courses Bill, 2021

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu moves Supreme Court against President's withholding of anti-NEET bill
evidence.snippet: In late 2025, the Tamil Nadu government moved the Supreme Court after the President withheld assent to the state's anti-NEET bill for the second time.

plan_existence.notes: NEET is a mandatory national exam. Tamil Nadu's attempts to abolish it via state legislation have been repeatedly blocked by the President and are currently under litigation in the Supreme Court.

legal.comments: The Supreme Court has repeatedly upheld the validity of NEET. State-level exemption requires Presidential assent under Article 254(2), which has been consistently denied.

administrative.comments: The state can continue to pass resolutions and file lawsuits, but it cannot unilaterally conduct medical admissions based on Class 12 marks without central/judicial approval.

timeline.comments: Dependent on a favorable Supreme Court verdict or a change in Union government policy, neither of which is guaranteed within a 5-year term.

political.comments: Extremely popular promise in Tamil Nadu; seen as a fight for social justice and state autonomy.

feasibility.overall_comments: While politically potent, this promise is legally stalled. The state has exhausted its legislative options, and the matter is now a high-stakes judicial and federal dispute with no clear path to immediate implementation.
key_risks: Adverse Supreme Court rulings
Continued refusal of Presidential assent
Uncertainty for medical aspirants during prolonged legal battles

search_queries: Tamil Nadu government stance on constituency delimitation 2026
Tamil Nadu two language policy vs NEP 2020 status
Tamil Nadu NEET abolition bill status 2026
One Nation One Election status India April 2026

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: It will be insisted that the respective state language be used as the language of the court in High Courts.
points.title: 9 State Language in High Courts

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Stalin reiterates demand for Supreme Court Bench in Chennai, use of Tamil in Madras High Court
evidence.snippet: Chief Minister M.K. Stalin on Saturday reiterated his request for opening a Bench of the Supreme Court in Chennai and allowing Tamil, the official language of the State, to be used in the Madras High Court.

plan_existence.notes: While the demand is decades old and supported by various TN governments, the official language of the Madras High Court remains English. Article 348(2) of the Constitution allows for the use of state languages with Presidential consent, which has not yet been granted for Tamil Nadu.

legal.comments: Requires the consent of the President of India under Article 348(2) of the Constitution and Section 7 of the Official Languages Act, 1963. Previous proposals have been rejected by the Supreme Court's full bench.

administrative.comments: Requires significant infrastructure for translation, training of non-Tamil speaking judges, and standardized legal terminology in Tamil.

timeline.comments: Implementation depends entirely on Union Government approval; if granted, administrative setup could take 2-3 years.

political.comments: High domestic support in Tamil Nadu across all political parties as a matter of linguistic pride and access to justice.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a standard political stance in Tamil Nadu. While administratively possible, it faces a significant legal bottleneck at the Union level and within the higher judiciary's institutional preferences.
key_risks: Rejection by the President or the Supreme Court of India
Challenges for judges from other states who do not know Tamil
Lack of standardized Tamil legal vocabulary for complex judgments

search_queries: Tamil language in Madras High Court status 2024 2025
Katchatheevu fishing rights legal status and TN government stance
Supreme Court bench in Chennai demand status
Cauvery Water Management Authority 2018 verdict implementation status TN

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: On behalf of TVK, continued pressure will be placed on the Union Government to establish a bench of the Supreme Court in Chennai.
points.title: 10 Supreme Court Bench in Chennai

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Regional Benches of the Supreme Court: A Constitutional Mandate Overdue?
evidence.snippet: The demand for regional benches of the Supreme Court in Chennai, Mumbai, and Kolkata is gaining momentum as pendency crosses 83,000 cases. Although Article 130 allows it, institutional concerns have stalled the move.

plan_existence.notes: The Law Commission of India (229th Report) recommended setting up regional benches, but the Supreme Court has historically resisted the move, citing the need to maintain the court's unitary character.

legal.comments: Under Article 130, the Chief Justice of India (CJI) must initiate the proposal with Presidential approval. The SC has repeatedly dismissed judicial petitions for regional benches.

administrative.comments: Setting up the physical infrastructure in Chennai is feasible, but managing a split registry and ensuring consistent jurisprudence across benches is a challenge.

timeline.comments: Unlikely to be achieved in 5 years given the consistent institutional opposition from the Supreme Court itself.

political.comments: Strong support from Southern states; however, it requires consensus at the Union level which has been elusive.

feasibility.overall_comments: A popular demand for 'Access to Justice' that faces a near-insurmountable institutional barrier within the judiciary. It is more of a pressure-tactic promise than an actionable state-level plan.
key_risks: Judicial opposition citing 'dilution of authority'
Constitutional amendment might be required if Article 130 is not invoked
Inter-state competition for the location of the Southern bench

search_queries: Tamil language in Madras High Court status 2024 2025
Katchatheevu fishing rights legal status and TN government stance
Supreme Court bench in Chennai demand status
Cauvery Water Management Authority 2018 verdict implementation status TN

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: TVK will secure Tamil Nadu's rightful share of Cauvery water as per the 2018 Supreme Court verdict. Immediate legal action will be taken against any obstacles created in obtaining Tamil Nadu's share of water. Measures will be taken to further strengthen Tamil Nadu's rights in the Cauvery Water Management Authority. Through continuous and systematic monitoring, we will not yield even a single drop of Tamil Nadu's rightful share of Cauvery water!
points.title: 11 Cauvery River Water Rights

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: Cauvery River Water Dispute, Cauvery Water Management Authority (CWMA)
evidence.snippet: In 2018, SC delivered its final verdict... The CWMA was created by the union government on 1 June 2018 to oversee the allocation.

plan_existence.notes: The legal framework and management bodies already exist. The promise focuses on enforcement and legal vigilance rather than a new scheme.

legal.comments: The 2018 SC verdict provides a solid legal foundation. The challenge is the legal defense against new projects like Mekedatu.

administrative.comments: The CWMA has the mandate but often struggles with enforcement during distress years when Karnataka refuses releases.

timeline.comments: This is an ongoing monitoring and legal process that spans the entire term.

political.comments: High risk of inter-state friction with Karnataka, which is a perennial political challenge regardless of the party in power.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a continuation of existing state policy. While the legal standing is strong, actual water receipt depends on monsoon patterns and the political climate in the neighboring state.
key_risks: Monsoon failure leading to 'distress years'
Non-compliance by Karnataka government
Construction of new dams (e.g., Mekedatu) by upstream state

Katchatheevu fishing rights legal status and TN government stance
Supreme Court bench in Chennai demand status
Cauvery Water Management Authority 2018 verdict implementation status TN

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: In line with the Katchatheevu agreement made between the governments of India and Sri Lanka, TVK will sincerely and emphatically pressure the Union Government to take appropriate measures to reclaim and preserve the traditional fishing rights of Indian and Tamil Nadu fishermen in the Gulf of Mannar. It will be strongly urged with the Union Government to completely prevent Tamil Nadu fishermen from being arrested by the Sri Lankan Navy.
points.title: 12 Katchatheevu Fishing Rights

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

evidence.title: TN Assembly passes resolution seeking retrieval of Katchatheevu island
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu Assembly on Wednesday (April 02, 2025) unanimously passed a resolution urging the Centre to retrieve Katchatheevu Island... to protect the traditional fishing rights.

plan_existence.notes: Katchatheevu was ceded to Sri Lanka via 1974 and 1976 agreements. While TN parties consistently demand its retrieval, it remains Sri Lankan territory under international law.

legal.comments: Retrieval involves reversing an international treaty, which the Union Government has stated is a settled matter. The SC is currently hearing petitions on its validity.

administrative.comments: State government has no jurisdiction over international borders or the Sri Lankan Navy; it can only act as a pressure group.

timeline.comments: Extremely unlikely to be resolved in 5 years as it requires bilateral renegotiation with a sovereign neighbor.

political.comments: Highly emotive and popular issue in coastal Tamil Nadu; serves as a strong tool for political mobilization.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is diplomatically and legally near-impossible for a state government to fulfill. It is a symbolic stance intended to signal solidarity with the fishing community.
key_risks: Diplomatic fallout with Sri Lanka
Legal rejection by the Supreme Court of India regarding treaty validity
Inability of the Union Government to act on state pressure due to geopolitical concerns

Katchatheevu fishing rights legal status and TN government stance
Supreme Court bench in Chennai demand status
Cauvery Water Management Authority 2018 verdict implementation status TN

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: On behalf of TVK, the Union Government will be urged to grant Indian citizenship to Sri Lankan Tamils who have migrated from Sri Lanka and are living in Tamil Nadu. Through this measure, it will be ensured that Sri Lankan Tamils receive all welfare schemes of the state government. Similarly, TVK will pressure the Union Government to take appropriate steps to protect the rights, cultural identity and possessions of Tamils living in Sri Lanka. TVK will continue to make appropriate efforts through the Union Government to eliminate ethnic discrimination prevailing in Sri Lanka.
points.title: 13 Justice for Sri Lankan Tamils

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

existing_scheme_names: Rehabilitation and Welfare of Non-Resident Tamils and Sri Lankan Tamils

evidence.title: Sri Lankan Tamils Schemes - TN Govt
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government provides comprehensive welfare support including clothing, free LPG, and cash allowances to Sri Lankan Tamil refugees in camps.

evidence.title: India exempts Eelam Tamil refugees from prosecution
evidence.snippet: Eelam Tamil refugees are exempted from penal provisions but remain barred from applying for citizenship under the current legal framework.

plan_existence.notes: While the TN government provides extensive welfare (housing, cash, education), citizenship is a Union subject. The CAA 2019 specifically excludes Sri Lankan Tamils from its fast-track pathway.

legal.comments: Citizenship is a Union subject under List I (Entry 17) of the Seventh Schedule; a state government has no constitutional authority to grant it.

administrative.comments: The state already has a department for refugee welfare, making the delivery of state-level schemes feasible if citizenship were granted.

timeline.comments: Granting citizenship depends entirely on the Union Government's legislative will, which has historically resisted this for Sri Lankan Tamils.

political.comments: High public and emotional resonance in Tamil Nadu; likely to receive strong local support but face central opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is legally infeasible for a state government to fulfill directly as citizenship is a central power. It functions primarily as a political pressure point rather than an actionable state policy.
key_risks: Constitutional conflict with Union Government
Exclusion from Central CAA framework
Diplomatic sensitivities with Sri Lanka

points.subsection_title: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights
points.text: TVK will continuously insist on abolishing the position of Governor, which continues as a vestige of colonial British India. Arrangements will be made for the state government itself to appoint Vice-Chancellors of universities.
points.title: 14 Action to Abolish Governor Appointments

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: A. Protection of Tamil Nadu's Finances and State Rights

existing_scheme_names: The Tamil Nadu Universities Laws (Amendment) Act, 2022

evidence.title: 10 Tamil Nadu bills become laws without governor's assent
evidence.snippet: The Supreme Court ruled that 10 bills, including those for VC appointments, are deemed laws after the Governor's delay was found illegal.

evidence.title: President returns University of Madras Bill
evidence.snippet: President Droupadi Murmu returned the University of Madras (Amendment Bill) in late 2025, stalling the state's move to change VC appointment rules.

plan_existence.notes: The state has already passed legislation to take over VC appointments, which is currently a subject of intense legal and constitutional dispute between the State, Governor, and President.

legal.comments: Abolishing the post of Governor requires a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368, necessitating a two-thirds majority in Parliament and ratification by half the states.

administrative.comments: While the state can manage VC appointments, the total removal of the Governor's office would require a complete overhaul of state executive procedures.

timeline.comments: Unlikely to be achieved in a 5-year term given the requirement for national-level constitutional change.

political.comments: Aligns with regionalist sentiments but faces insurmountable opposition from any central government wishing to maintain federal oversight.

feasibility.overall_comments: The proposal to abolish the Governor is constitutionally impossible for a state party to implement. The VC appointment aspect is already in a state of legal flux with the Supreme Court and President.
key_risks: Constitutional deadlock
Judicial strike-down of state-led VC appointments
Conflict with UGC regulations

points.text: To proclaim and protect the history of Tamils, who possess a unique heritage and pride in world history, Tamil cultural sites will be restored at a cost of Rs. 500 crore. Traditional Tamil cultural arts including silambam, kummi, kolattam, and Thanjavur painting will be revived. International-level cultural exhibitions will be held to make the historical pride and cultural identities of Tamils famous worldwide.
points.title: 1 Budget for Tamil Heritage and Civilization

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 1 Budget for Tamil Heritage and Civilization

existing_scheme_names: Department of Art and Culture Schemes

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Budget 2024-25: Culture and Archaeology
evidence.snippet: The 2024-25 budget allocated funds for the archaeology department and cultural exhibitions, though the specific Rs 500 crore for restoration is a new target.

evidence.title: Kalaignar Sports Kit Scheme
evidence.snippet: The government provides sports equipment, including for Silambam, to 12,000 village panchayats.

plan_existence.notes: Existing departments promote these arts, but the proposed Rs 500 crore budget represents a significant scale-up compared to current annual allocations for art and culture.

fiscal.comments: Rs 500 crore is a manageable outlay for the Tamil Nadu state budget, which already allocates similar amounts for large-scale cultural projects like film cities.

legal.comments: No legal barriers; the state has full authority over the promotion of local culture and heritage.

administrative.comments: The existing Department of Art and Culture and the Tourism Department provide a ready mechanism for implementation.

timeline.comments: Restoration projects and exhibitions can be executed within a 5-year term.

political.comments: Culturally popular and politically safe; promotes 'Tamil Pride' which is a central theme in state politics.

feasibility.overall_comments: A highly feasible and standard welfare/heritage promise. The specific budget allocation is realistic and the administrative machinery is already in place.
key_risks: Under-utilization of funds
Quality control in restoration
Commercial viability of exhibitions

points.text: TVK will support the Archaeology Department in continuing excavations at 10 sites: Keezhadi, Adichanallur, Kodumanal, Mayiladumparai, Sivakalai, Azhagankulam, Arikamedu, Saluvankuppam, Kaveripattinam, and Poompuhar. Efforts will be made to obtain UNESCO World Heritage Site recognition for both Keezhadi and Adichanallur. To help future generations learn our history, school student tours to these sites will be organised at government expense.
points.title: 2 Archaeological Excavations

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu State Department of Archaeology Excavations

evidence.title: Keezhadi excavation site - Wikipedia
evidence.snippet: The tenth phase of excavation began in June 2024. Excavations are carried out by the Tamil Nadu State Department of Archaeology.

evidence.title: TN Budget 2024: Rs 25.65 Crore for Archaeology
evidence.snippet: Funds were allocated for excavations in eight locations including Keezhadi, Vembakottai, and others in 2024-25.

plan_existence.notes: Excavations at most of these sites are already ongoing under the State Archaeology Department. The UNESCO recognition and state-funded student tours are specific additions.

fiscal.comments: The cost of excavations and student tours is relatively low (tens of crores), making it very affordable for the state.

legal.comments: State has authority over archaeology; however, UNESCO recognition requires a complex nomination process through the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI).

administrative.comments: The State Archaeology Department is highly active and capable of managing these sites and tours.

timeline.comments: Excavations and tours can be scaled immediately; UNESCO recognition typically takes much longer than 5 years.

political.comments: Strongly aligns with the 'Sangam Landscape' and 'Ancient Tamil' narrative popular in TN politics.

feasibility.overall_comments: A very practical and popular promise that builds on existing state momentum. The UNESCO goal is aspirational, but the core activities are already operational.
key_risks: Delays in UNESCO nomination by Central Govt
Preservation of artifacts during mass student visits
Site maintenance costs

search_queries: Tamil Nadu budget 2024-25 archaeology and culture allocation
Tamil Nadu government welfare schemes for Sri Lankan Tamils 2024
Tamil Nadu Universities Laws Amendment Bill 2022 status VC appointment
Indian citizenship for Sri Lankan Tamil refugees Tamil Nadu current status 2024 2025
Tamil Nadu bill to abolish Governor post status
Keezhadi Adichanallur UNESCO world heritage site status 2024

points.text: To proclaim the thousands of years of Tamil history, an international-class 'Tamil Civilization Museum' will be established in Madurai.
points.title: 3 'Tamil Civilization Museum' in Madurai

evidence.title: Keeladi Museum: Stalin inaugurates Sangam-era site near Madurai
evidence.snippet: The Keeladi Heritage Museum, showcasing the Vaigai River Civilization, was inaugurated in 2023 in Sivaganga district, approximately 13km from Madurai.

evidence.title: Epigraphy museum to come up in Madurai
evidence.snippet: A dedicated museum focusing on epigraphy and inscriptions will be set up at the World Tamil Sangam in Madurai.

plan_existence.notes: While the world-class Keeladi Museum exists nearby in Sivaganga, a specific 'Tamil Civilization Museum' within Madurai city limits is a distinct proposal. Existing facilities include a small government museum and a planned epigraphy museum.

fiscal.comments: The Keeladi Museum was built for approximately ₹18.42 crore. An 'international-class' facility might cost ₹50-100 crore, which is well within the state's capital expenditure capacity for culture.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; the state has full authority to establish museums under the Department of Archaeology.

administrative.comments: The Tamil Nadu Department of Archaeology has demonstrated capacity with the Keeladi project. Land acquisition in Madurai city would be the primary task.

timeline.comments: Large-scale museums typically take 2-3 years from design to inauguration, making it feasible within a 5-year term.

political.comments: High political value as it taps into Tamil identity and pride, with likely broad public support.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible project that builds on the successful 'Keeladi' model to centralize Tamil heritage tourism in Madurai.
key_risks: Land acquisition delays in urban Madurai
Potential duplication of exhibits with the existing Keeladi Museum
Cost overruns for 'international-class' specifications

search_queries: Keeladi Museum Madurai location and status
Tamil Nadu financial assistance for folk instruments 15000
Tamil Nadu Young Artists Encouragement Scheme 25000 grant 1000 artists
Tamil Civilization Museum Madurai existing or planned
Folk arts research and training centres Coimbatore Thanjavur Tirunelveli existing
Tamil Nadu Folk Artist Welfare Board 100 days employment scheme

points.text: Folk Artists Livelihood Protection Scheme: To ensure that registered folk artists get 100 days of employment per year, opportunities to showcase their artistic skills will be created through government functions and events. Additionally, financial assistance of Rs. 15,000 will be provided to help them buy the necessary instruments.
points.title: 4 Folk Artist Welfare Improvement

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Folk Artistes Welfare Board
Scheme for Financial Assistance to Folk Artists

evidence.title: TN govt extends aid to state Kalaimamani awardees, folk artists
evidence.snippet: The state government provided financial assistance of Rs 10,000 each to 500 folk artists for the purchase of instruments and costumes in 2023.

evidence.title: Scheme of Extending Financial Assistance to Folk Artists
evidence.snippet: The scheme provides a sum of ₹2,000 to individual folk artists and ₹6,000 for cultural troupes for instruments.

plan_existence.notes: The 100-day employment guarantee for artists is a new concept mimicking MGNREGS. The ₹15,000 instrument grant is an expansion of the existing ₹2,000-₹10,000 assistance.

fiscal.comments: With roughly 40,000 registered folk artists, a ₹15,000 grant would cost ₹60 crore. The 100-day employment cost depends on the daily wage; at ₹300/day, it could exceed ₹1,200 crore annually if fully utilized.

legal.comments: Welfare boards have the statutory power to implement such schemes.

administrative.comments: Extremely difficult to track and provide 100 days of 'artistic' work for thousands of artists through government events alone. Likely to face ghost-beneficiary issues.

timeline.comments: The financial grant can be rolled out quickly; the employment scheme would require a complex booking/tracking portal.

political.comments: Very popular among the rural artist community, a key cultural demographic.

feasibility.overall_comments: The financial grant is highly feasible, but the 100-day employment guarantee is administratively ambitious and lacks a clear delivery mechanism.
key_risks: Administrative burden of scheduling 100 days of work per artist
Fiscal strain if artist registration surges
Potential for corruption in work-day certification

search_queries: Keeladi Museum Madurai location and status
Tamil Nadu financial assistance for folk instruments 15000
Tamil Nadu Young Artists Encouragement Scheme 25000 grant 1000 artists
Tamil Civilization Museum Madurai existing or planned
Folk arts research and training centres Coimbatore Thanjavur Tirunelveli existing
Tamil Nadu Folk Artist Welfare Board 100 days employment scheme

points.text: Young Artists Encouragement Scheme: To encourage emerging young folk artists, a grant of Rs. 25,000 per year will be given to 1,000 young artists.
points.title: Young Artists Encouragement Scheme

existing_scheme_names: Scheme of Encouragement to Young Artists
Scheme to Award Scholarship to Young Artists

evidence.title: Scheme of Encouragement to Young Artists - myScheme
evidence.snippet: The scheme gives an opportunity to talented young artists to perform in cultural programmes with remuneration in the range of ₹5,000 to ₹10,000.

plan_existence.notes: Existing schemes focus on performance remuneration (up to ₹10k) or fine arts scholarships (up to ₹5k). A flat ₹25,000 annual grant for 1,000 artists is a significant increase in benefit amount.

fiscal.comments: The total annual cost is ₹2.5 crore (1,000 artists × ₹25,000). This is a negligible amount in the state budget.

legal.comments: No legal constraints.

administrative.comments: The Department of Art and Culture already has selection committees for similar schemes. Scaling to 1,000 beneficiaries is straightforward.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within the first year of government.

political.comments: Positive reception as it targets youth and cultural preservation.

feasibility.overall_comments: A low-cost, high-impact welfare promise that is easily implementable through existing departmental structures.
key_risks: Subjectivity in the selection of the 1,000 'emerging' artists
Limited reach (only 1,000 artists) may lead to dissatisfaction among those excluded

search_queries: Keeladi Museum Madurai location and status
Tamil Nadu financial assistance for folk instruments 15000
Tamil Nadu Young Artists Encouragement Scheme 25000 grant 1000 artists
Tamil Civilization Museum Madurai existing or planned
Folk arts research and training centres Coimbatore Thanjavur Tirunelveli existing
Tamil Nadu Folk Artist Welfare Board 100 days employment scheme

points.text: Folk arts research and training centres will be established in Coimbatore (karagattam, kummi, oyilattam), Thanjavur (Bharatanatyam, therukoothu, parai), and Tirunelveli (silambam, kambattam), where free training will be provided to interested students.
points.title: 5 Folk Arts Research and Training Centres

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 5 Folk Arts Research and Training Centres

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Dr. J. Jayalalithaa Music and Fine Arts University

evidence.title: Folklore Resources and Research Centre St.Xavier's College
evidence.snippet: The FRRC in Palayamkottai (Tirunelveli) conducts certificate courses in folkloristics and archives folk materials.

plan_existence.notes: While Thanjavur has the SZCC and Tamil University, and Tirunelveli has private/academic research centers, dedicated government-run 'Folk Arts Research and Training Centres' offering free training in these specific regional arts do not currently exist as a unified state project.

fiscal.comments: Establishing three centers would involve moderate capital expenditure (est. ₹15-30 crore total) and recurring costs for trainers. Feasible for the state budget.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles.

administrative.comments: Can be managed by the Directorate of Art and Culture. Finding qualified 'masters' for traditional arts to serve as trainers is the main task.

timeline.comments: Centers can be established in existing government buildings or new small-scale facilities within 2 years.

political.comments: Strong regional appeal by mapping specific arts to their traditional geographic hubs (e.g., Silambam in Tirunelveli).

feasibility.overall_comments: A well-structured proposal that institutionalizes traditional knowledge and provides accessible vocational training in culture.
key_risks: Difficulty in standardizing curriculum for oral-tradition folk arts
Ensuring consistent student enrollment in specialized centers
Maintaining the quality of 'free' training

search_queries: Keeladi Museum Madurai location and status
Tamil Nadu financial assistance for folk instruments 15000
Tamil Nadu Young Artists Encouragement Scheme 25000 grant 1000 artists
Tamil Civilization Museum Madurai existing or planned
Folk arts research and training centres Coimbatore Thanjavur Tirunelveli existing
Tamil Nadu Folk Artist Welfare Board 100 days employment scheme

points.text: The following schemes will be implemented to revive, encourage and develop Tamil Nadu's traditional sports: *A Tamil Nadu Rural Olympics series will be held to take Tamil traditional sports to the global stage. *Rs. 5 lakh financial assistance will be given by the government to conduct jallikattu events. Rs. 10 lakh will be given to the family of a bull-tamer who dies during jallikattu. Similarly, Rs. 2 lakh will be given to an injured participant. Further, measures will be taken to promote jallikattu as an international sport. *Rekla racing grounds will be built in districts such as Coimbatore and Salem where rekla races are predominantly held. *Rs. 5 crore will be provided annually to conduct silambam competitions at the international level. One thousand silambam training centres will be established across Tamil Nadu.
points.title: 6 Traditional Sports Development Scheme

existing_scheme_names: Chief Minister's Public Relief Fund (Jallikattu compensation)

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu CM Trophy Games 2024
evidence.snippet: The CM Trophy includes traditional sports like Silambam and Kabaddi across various age groups and levels.

evidence.title: Compensation for Jallikattu victims
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government currently provides ex-gratia payments (typically Rs. 3 lakh) from the CM Public Relief Fund for deaths during Jallikattu.

plan_existence.notes: Traditional sports are already integrated into the CM Trophy. Jallikattu compensation exists but the proposed amounts (Rs. 10 lakh) represent a significant increase from current discretionary ex-gratia payments.

fiscal.comments: The specific outlays (Rs. 5 crore for Silambam, Rs. 5-10 lakh for Jallikattu aid) are relatively small within the state's sports and welfare budget.

legal.comments: Jallikattu and traditional sports are legally protected under the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (Tamil Nadu Amendment) Act, 2017, upheld by the Supreme Court.

administrative.comments: The Sports Development Authority of Tamil Nadu (SDAT) has the existing infrastructure to scale these competitions.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately through executive orders and budget allocations.

political.comments: Strong cultural resonance; traditional sports are a major part of Tamil identity politics.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible and builds on existing sports infrastructure. The fiscal burden is manageable, and the political/cultural alignment is very strong.
key_risks: Safety management in Jallikattu
Standardization of rules for 'Rural Olympics'
Maintenance of new Rekla grounds

points.text: A special scheme will be introduced with the aim of making Thirukkural a functional part of every student's life. Students who fully learn 300 kurals will be given cash prizes and educational scholarships; those who fully learn all 1,330 kurals will be given global cultural experience, state-level recognition and long-term educational support. Further, to build leadership rooted in Tamil heritage values, Thirukkural will be introduced as a special subject in schools and business education institutions.
points.title: 7 Thirukkural Development Scheme

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Government Thirukkural Awards
evidence.snippet: The government provides a cash prize of Rs. 10,000 to students who recite all 1,330 couplets of Thirukkural.

plan_existence.notes: A scheme already exists for students reciting all 1,330 kurals. The promise expands this to include 300-kural milestones and 'global cultural experience' which is a new qualitative addition.

legal.comments: State has the authority to modify school curriculum and introduce special subjects under the State Board.

administrative.comments: The School Education Department and Tamil Development Department can manage the awards and curriculum changes.

timeline.comments: Curriculum changes and scholarship rollouts can be achieved within 1-2 academic years.

political.comments: Thirukkural is universally revered in Tamil Nadu; no political opposition expected.

feasibility.overall_comments: A culturally popular and administratively straightforward expansion of existing recognition programs. The 'global experience' component requires clear eligibility criteria.
key_risks: Standardization of 'global cultural experience'
Academic load on students
Verification of recitation authenticity

points.text: To honour the sacrifice of the language struggle martyrs, the annual pension given to their families will be increased to Rs. 2,50,000 each. To remember the language struggle martyrs, speech and essay competitions will be held for students.
points.title: 8 Pensions for Language Struggle Martyrs

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Pension for Language Martyrs
evidence.snippet: In 2023, the monthly pension for Tamil language martyrs' families was increased to Rs. 6,000 plus a dearness allowance.

plan_existence.notes: The current pension is approximately Rs. 72,000 per year (plus DA). The promise to increase it to Rs. 2,50,000 per year is a nearly 3.5x increase.

fiscal.comments: The number of beneficiaries is small (estimated under 600 families), making the total annual outlay (approx. Rs. 15 crore) very low for the state budget.

legal.comments: Purely an executive decision to increase pension amounts under existing state rules.

administrative.comments: The Tamil Development and Information Department already has the list of beneficiaries and the disbursement mechanism.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented in the first cabinet meeting.

political.comments: High symbolic value for Tamil nationalist sentiment with negligible cost.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible, low-cost, and high-impact political move that honors a specific historical constituency.
key_risks: Verification of legal heirs for older cases

points.text: With the arrival of AI technology, every field has adapted accordingly. However, AI technology is based largely on English. Designing and using this technology in Tamil has become a necessity of the times. Therefore, a dedicated Tamil AI model will be developed in partnership with leading universities. This service will be provided free of charge to all our country's people. It will be provided to foreigners on the basis of a commercial licence.
points.title: 9 Tamil Artificial Intelligence

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Government to develop Tamil LLM
evidence.snippet: The TN government announced a project to develop a Tamil Large Language Model (LLM) with an initial allocation of Rs. 2 crore.

plan_existence.notes: The government has already initiated a Tamil LLM project. The promise expands this into a 'dedicated Tamil AI model' with a specific commercial licensing model for foreigners.

legal.comments: Intellectual property and licensing frameworks for state-funded AI models would need careful legal drafting.

administrative.comments: TNeGA (Tamil Nadu e-Governance Agency) and ELCOT have the capacity to oversee this in partnership with institutions like IIT Madras.

timeline.comments: Developing a high-quality, foundational AI model is a multi-year technical endeavor.

political.comments: Aligns with the 'Digital Tamil' and 'Tamil Pride' narratives.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically ambitious but grounded in existing state initiatives. The commercial licensing aspect is a novel revenue-generating idea for state-owned tech.
key_risks: High computational costs
Data privacy and bias in AI training
Competition from global tech giants' Tamil models

points.text: Sufficient funds will be allocated to create digital archives to protect Tamil Nadu's art, culture and heritage. Under the Tamil Nadu Digital Museum project, efforts will be made to document Tamil Nadu's history, culture and heritage through AR (Augmented Reality), VR (Virtual Reality) and Hologram technology.
points.title: 10 Digital Archives of Tamil Heritage

evidence.title: Tamil Virtual Academy
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Virtual Academy (TVA) provides digital library services and has been involved in digitizing palm-leaf manuscripts and heritage artifacts.

evidence.title: TN Budget 2024-25: Digital Museum to be set up in Chennai
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government announced the establishment of a world-class digital museum in Chennai to showcase the state's rich history and culture.

plan_existence.notes: The state already has a dedicated body (TVA) for digital archiving and has recently funded a major digital museum project in Chennai. This promise expands the scale and technological scope (AR/VR/Holograms) of existing efforts.

legal.comments: No legal or constitutional barriers to digitizing state heritage.

administrative.comments: The Tamil Virtual Academy and the Department of Archaeology have the existing institutional capacity to execute this.

timeline.comments: Digital archiving and AR/VR development for 10 sites can be completed within a 5-year term.

political.comments: High public support for initiatives promoting Tamil identity and heritage.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it builds on the existing infrastructure of the Tamil Virtual Academy and recent budget allocations for digital museums and AR/VR heritage projects.
key_risks: High technology maintenance costs
Digital obsolescence of hardware
Accuracy of historical documentation

points.text: TVK's 'Tamil Nadu Responsible Tourism' is presented as a joyful and creative tourism scheme that treats tourism not merely as entertainment but includes eco-tourism, heritage tourism, coastal tourism and cultural tourism. 25 percent of the revenue from this will be spent on improving the livelihoods of the respective local people.
points.title: 1 Tamil Nadu Responsible Tourism

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Tourism Policy 2023
evidence.snippet: The policy focuses on sustainable tourism, eco-tourism, and community participation but does not mandate a specific 25% revenue-sharing model.

plan_existence.notes: While the state has a comprehensive tourism policy focusing on sustainability, the specific mandate to spend 25% of revenue on local livelihoods is a new fiscal amendment to the existing framework.

legal.comments: Requires new financial rules or legislation to mandate revenue transfers to local community funds.

administrative.comments: Tracking revenue at the site level and ensuring it reaches 'local people' without leakage is administratively complex.

timeline.comments: Policy can be enacted quickly, but the distribution mechanism will take time to stabilize.

political.comments: Likely popular with local voters in tourist hubs, though may face resistance from centralized tourism departments.

feasibility.overall_comments: While the focus on responsible tourism aligns with state policy, the 25% revenue-sharing mandate is a complex fiscal promise that requires a robust mechanism to define and distribute funds to 'local people.'
key_risks: Defining 'local people' for revenue distribution
Administrative leakage
Impact on tourism department's operational budget

points.text: The following initiatives will be implemented to attract tourists by centering Tamil culture: Folk art performances will be held every evening at 10 major tourist sites. Rural tourism centres providing a village life experience will be created. Night markets selling handicrafts made by local artisans will be set up near all cultural tourism sites. A Tamil Cultural Festival will be held during tourist seasons including the summer holidays.
points.title: 2 Archaeological Excavations

evidence.title: Department of Art and Culture - Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: The department organizes 'Namma Ooru Thiruvizha' to promote folk arts and supports various cultural festivals across the state.

plan_existence.notes: Folk art festivals and rural tourism are existing initiatives. Night markets are currently being piloted in cities like Chennai; expanding them to all cultural sites is a significant scale-up.

legal.comments: No legal barriers to promoting culture or setting up markets.

administrative.comments: The Department of Art and Culture and local municipalities have the capacity to organize these events.

timeline.comments: These initiatives can be rolled out incrementally across the state within 5 years.

political.comments: Strong public and political support for promoting local arts and handicrafts.

feasibility.overall_comments: A practical set of initiatives that expands on current folk art promotion and urban night market pilots, though scaling night markets to all cultural sites will require significant local infrastructure.
key_risks: Commercialization of traditional arts
Waste management at night markets
Seasonal nature of tourism affecting rural centers

points.text: A Tamil Nadu Spiritual Tourism scheme will be developed along four major spiritual routes: *The Arupadai Veedu Tourism Scheme covering the spiritual sites of Palani, Thirupparamkunram, Thiruchendur, Swamimalai, Thiruthani and Pazhamudircholai. *The Shaivite Temples Tourism Scheme covering Chidambaram, Tiruvannamalai, Kumbakonam and Thiruvaiyaru. *The Vaishnavite Holy Sites Tourism Scheme covering Srirangam, Thirukoshtiyur and Alwar Thirunagari. *The Inter-faith Harmony Tourism Scheme covering holy sites of all our religions, including Nagore Dargah, Velankanni and Kanyakumari.
points.title: 3 Spiritual Tourism Scheme

points.text: Rest houses for pilgrims will be built at Tiruvannamalai, Madurai, Rameswaram, Palani, Velankanni and Nagore — major spiritual worship centres for all religions.
points.title: 4 Rest Houses for Pilgrims

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Tourism Development Corporation (TTDC) Hotels

evidence.title: CM Stalin lays foundation for new infrastructure projects at 26 HR&CE temples
evidence.snippet: In June 2025, the TN government launched 49 new projects worth ₹217.98 crore, including facilities for devotees in various districts like Palani and Madurai.

evidence.title: Pilgrim's palace at Srirangam
evidence.snippet: The Yatri Nivas at Srirangam caters to 1,000 pilgrims with 612 rooms and family cottages, built at an estimate of ₹47.08 crore.

plan_existence.notes: The Government of Tamil Nadu, through the HR&CE and Tourism departments, already builds and operates 'Yatri Nivas' and rest houses at major temple towns. Recent 2025 initiatives specifically targeted Palani and Madurai. This promise extends the model to multi-religious sites like Velankanni and Nagore, which are major non-Hindu spiritual hubs.

fiscal.comments: The HR&CE department has substantial surplus funds from temple revenues (over ₹3,000 crore annually), and the Tourism department has a dedicated budget for infrastructure. A typical large-scale Yatri Nivas costs approximately ₹40-50 crore.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; the state has clear authority to build public amenities on government or temple-held land.

administrative.comments: The HR&CE and TTDC have established mechanisms for constructing and managing such facilities.

timeline.comments: Construction of 6 rest houses can be completed within a 3-4 year window.

political.comments: Highly popular across all religious groups; promotes communal harmony by including Christian and Muslim sites.

feasibility.overall_comments: A highly feasible and standard infrastructure promise that builds on existing successful models of pilgrim housing in the state. The inclusion of multi-religious sites is a strategic expansion of current policy.
key_risks: Land acquisition delays in congested temple towns
Maintenance quality post-construction
Potential cost overruns in high-demand areas

search_queries: Tamil Nadu Tourism Policy 2023 digital experience AR VR cultural sites
HR&CE Tamil Nadu new pilgrim rest houses 2024 2025
Keezhadi Adichanallur Kodumanal Gangaikondacholapuram AR VR tourism Tamil Nadu
Tamil Nadu government pilgrim rest houses Tiruvannamalai Madurai Rameswaram Palani Velankanni Nagore

points.text: At cultural sites such as Keezhadi, Adichanallur, Kodumanal and Gangaikondacholapuram, the Tamil urban civilisation experience will be recreated for tourists through AR (Augmented Reality) and VR (Virtual Reality).
points.title: 5 Tamil Cultural Tourism Scheme

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Tourism Policy 2023
evidence.snippet: The policy aims to provide 'extraordinary experiences' leveraging history and envisions virtual Tourist Information Centres and immersive storytelling.

evidence.title: Centre's tourism strategy favours Adichanallur
evidence.snippet: The Union Budget 2026-27 announced the development of Adichanallur as an experiential cultural destination with immersive storytelling technologies.

plan_existence.notes: The Tamil Nadu Tourism Policy 2023 already sets the framework for digital and experiential tourism. Furthermore, the Central Government has specifically funded Adichanallur for similar AR/VR interventions. This promise expands the scope to include Keezhadi, Kodumanal, and Gangaikondacholapuram under a unified state scheme.

fiscal.comments: AR/VR content creation and hardware (headsets, kiosks) are expensive. While the state has a ₹20,000 crore investment target for tourism, high-tech projects require consistent funding for tech refreshes.

legal.comments: No legal constraints; the state archaeology department (TNSDA) has jurisdiction over these sites.

administrative.comments: Requires specialized technical expertise and private sector partnerships. Government departments often struggle with the long-term maintenance of high-tech hardware in rural/dusty environments.

timeline.comments: AR/VR content for 4 sites can be developed and deployed within 2-3 years.

political.comments: Strong political appeal as it promotes 'Tamil Pride' and the antiquity of Tamil civilization.

feasibility.overall_comments: A modern, tech-forward promise that aligns perfectly with existing state and central policies. The primary challenge will be the technical upkeep of the equipment at remote archaeological sites.
key_risks: High maintenance cost of VR hardware
Rapid obsolescence of digital content
Poor internet connectivity at remote excavation sites affecting AR performance

HR&CE Tamil Nadu new pilgrim rest houses 2024 2025
Keezhadi Adichanallur Kodumanal Gangaikondacholapuram AR VR tourism Tamil Nadu
Tamil Nadu government pilgrim rest houses Tiruvannamalai Madurai Rameswaram Palani Velankanni Nagore
